title.en: Choosing a Provider: Claude vs GPT vs Gemini by Workload

description.en: Replace vendor debates with a repeatable selection method: workload-specific criteria, weighted scoring, and a multi-provider posture that treats the choice as routing, not marriage.

story_variants.en:
## The Wrong Question

"Which AI vendor is best?" is the wrong question, and every month spent debating it is a month of shipped-nothing. The right question is narrower and answerable: **"Which provider best fits *this workload*, under *our constraints*, as of *this quarter*?"** Three qualifiers, each load-bearing: workloads differ (a coding agent and a call-center summarizer stress different capabilities), constraints differ (your cloud, your compliance regime, your budget), and the answer *expires* (leaderboards reshuffle with every model generation).

This lesson turns the previous twelve into a decision method.

## Centers of Gravity, Honestly Stated

All three providers are excellent; all three will claim to be excellent at everything. Their genuine centers of gravity, as consistently reported by production teams:

| Workload | Often shortlisted first | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Agentic coding, complex tool loops | Claude | Reputation for careful multi-step work; Claude Code & MCP ecosystem |
| Hard math/science reasoning | OpenAI o-series | Purpose-built reasoning with tunable effort |
| Massive-context & video analysis | Gemini | Million-token class windows, native multimodality |
| High-volume, cost-dominated pipelines | Gemini Flash tiers / mini-tier GPT / Haiku | Economy tiers are everyone's knife fight |
| Long-form writing with reliable tone | Claude | Frequent qualitative preference in evals |
| Broadest ecosystem & integrations | OpenAI | Largest developer community and tooling |
| Suite-embedded workforce AI | Decided by your suite | Copilot vs Gemini follows M365 vs Workspace |

Treat this table as *priors*, not verdicts. Priors tell you which two providers to evaluate first — your evals tell you which one wins.

> **Callout:** Beware capability claims more than one model-generation old, including this table's. The half-life of "vendor X is clearly better at Y" is roughly six months. Method outlives facts; that is why this lesson teaches method.

## The Constraint Filter Comes First

Before scoring anything, apply hard constraints — they eliminate options cheaply:

- **Cloud gravity**: an AWS-standardized org gets Claude via Bedrock trivially, OpenAI models via Azure only; a GCP org gets Gemini *and* Claude in Vertex side by side. Sometimes the cloud decides for you.
- **Compliance regime**: data residency, sector certifications, and audit requirements disqualify specific channels (not necessarily vendors — often the direct API fails a constraint the cloud reseller satisfies).
- **Existing spend commitments**: cloud commit drawdown can outweigh a 20% list-price difference.

Only candidates surviving the filter deserve scoring effort.

## Weighted Scoring: The Deciding Machine

You built the pieces already — lesson 2's scorecard, lesson 3's cost model. Provider selection composes them:

1. **Define axes for the workload** — e.g., task accuracy (from your private evals), ecosystem/integration fit, cost at projected volume, latency, governance fit.
2. **Weight the axes** by what the workload actually punishes: a customer-facing chatbot weights latency heavily; a nightly batch pipeline weights it near zero.
3. **Score each surviving provider** per axis — eval results for accuracy, cost-model output for cost, architecture review for fit.
4. **Compute, rank, document.** The documentation matters: a decision record with weights, scores and dates makes the choice *re-litigable with data* when the landscape shifts, instead of re-fought with opinions.

The weighted sum is deliberately boring math — `sum(score[axis] * weight[axis])` — and that boringness is the feature. Executives can audit it, engineers can rerun it, and nobody's charisma changes the answer.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Weight-rigging — adjusting weights until the politically preferred vendor wins. The tell is weights that change between evaluations without a workload change. Fix the weights *before* scoring, in writing. (A second pitfall: scoring all three vendors on axes where one didn't survive the constraint filter. Filter first; score second.)

## Multi-Provider Is the Norm, Not the Hedge

Surveys of enterprise AI adoption consistently find most organizations using **two or more** frontier providers in production. Not indecision — architecture. Different workloads genuinely have different winners; a routing layer (the abstraction this track keeps prescribing) makes multi-provider cheap; and concentration risk is real (pricing changes, capacity incidents, deprecations). The professional posture: **a primary provider for depth** (tooling, staff expertise, negotiated rates) **plus a secondary for fit and leverage** — with your gateway making the difference invisible to application teams.

What multi-provider does *not* mean: building every feature three times, or maintaining N prompt libraries. Prompts port with modest effort; evals port almost unchanged — and your eval suite is precisely what makes switching *safe* rather than scary.

## The Challenge

You will implement the deciding machine end-to-end in miniature: three providers scored on three axes with workload weights (mock fit ratings, clearly labeled), ranked deterministically, recommendation printed. The numbers are toys; the shape is exactly what your architecture review board should see attached to every provider decision — and exactly what you should rerun the week after the next model generation ships.

description.en: The selection pipeline: filter by hard constraints, score what survives, document the decision, encode it in routing.

left.label: Debate 'which vendor is best' until consensus emerges

right.label: Fix weights in writing, score per workload, rank, and record the decision with a date

description.en: Weighted scoring is boring math — and boringness is the feature: auditable, rerunnable, charisma-proof.

explanation.en: Per-axis fit ratings come from evals, cost models and architecture review — never from vibes.
The weighted sum: fixed weights, written down before scoring, immune to weight-rigging.
Sorting by score produces the ranking; the top entry is the recommendation for THIS workload.

description.en: A close race decided by workload weights — for a different workload, the same method flips the order. That's the point.
Provider selection as a repeatable loop with an expiry date, not a one-time marriage.

instruction.en: Build the provider-selection scorer end-to-end. Complete score(provider_fit, weights): return the sum of provider_fit[axis] * weights[axis] over every axis in weights, rounded to 1 decimal. Then rank the three providers in SCORES from highest to lowest and print EXACTLY four lines: '<provider>: <score>' per provider in descending score order, then 'recommendation=<top provider>'. The fit ratings are mock numbers for one hypothetical workload — the method, not the numbers, is what you are learning to defend in an architecture review.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
SCORES = {  # mock workload-fit ratings (1-10) for ONE hypothetical workload
    "claude": {"reasoning": 9, "ecosystem": 7, "cost": 6},
    "gpt": {"reasoning": 8, "ecosystem": 9, "cost": 6},
    "gemini": {"reasoning": 8, "ecosystem": 8, "cost": 8},
}

WEIGHTS = {"reasoning": 0.5, "ecosystem": 0.3, "cost": 0.2}

def score(provider_fit, weights):
    # TODO: weighted sum over the axes in weights, rounded to 1 decimal
    pass

# TODO: rank providers by score, highest first
# TODO: print "<provider>: <score>" per provider in that order
# TODO: print "recommendation=<top provider>"

en: score is: round(sum(provider_fit[a] * weights[a] for a in weights), 1).
Build pairs = [(name, score(fit, WEIGHTS)) for name, fit in SCORES.items()] then sort by the score descending.
gemini wins 8.0 vs 7.9 vs 7.8 — cost weight tips a close race. Print the top name after the loop.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert score(SCORES["claude"], WEIGHTS) == 7.8
assert score(SCORES["gpt"], WEIGHTS) == 7.9
assert score(SCORES["gemini"], WEIGHTS) == 8.0
assert score({"reasoning": 10, "ecosystem": 10, "cost": 10}, WEIGHTS) == 10.0

q.en: Why is 'which AI vendor is best?' the wrong question for enterprises?

en: Because vendors refuse to be compared
Because the answer depends on the workload, the organization's constraints, and the date — all three vary
Because benchmarks are illegal to cite
Because all models are functionally identical

q.en: What should happen BEFORE any provider scoring effort?

en: Applying hard constraints — cloud gravity, compliance, spend commitments — to eliminate non-viable options cheaply
Signing an LOI with the preferred vendor
Building the full application on each provider

q.en: What is 'weight-rigging' and how is it prevented?

en: Vendors inflating their own benchmark weights; prevented by regulation
Adjusting scorecard weights until the politically preferred vendor wins; prevented by fixing weights in writing before scoring
Overweighting cost in every evaluation; prevented by ignoring cost
A GPU load-balancing technique; prevented by autoscaling

q.en: Why do most enterprises end up multi-provider in production?

en: Contracts require using all major vendors
Different workloads have genuinely different winners, routing layers make it cheap, and concentration risk is real
